headline: The 2025 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball Draft: Where Champions Were Built and Empires Crumbled
subheadline: One manager turned Rhys Hoskins into a Round 8 heist, another reached for Bo Bichette like it was 2021, and the defending champs reminded everyone why they're the scariest team in the league.
body:
**I. The War Room Opens**

There's a scene in *Moneyball* where Billy Beane is pacing the halls during the draft, too nervous to sit still, convinced that every pick is either the foundation of a dynasty or the first domino in a catastrophic collapse. That's what it felt like watching the 2025 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball draft unfold on March 22nd — fourteen managers, fourteen different philosophies, and approximately 210 opportunities to either look like a genius or become the punchline of league group chat memes for the next calendar year.

This draft had everything: a defending champion loading up like they were playing with house money (because they were), a team literally named the **Buttville Skidmarks** making surprisingly competent decisions, and enough reaches to make you wonder if some managers were drafting with their monitors turned off. We saw **Rhys Hoskins** fall to Round 8 like he was radioactive. We watched someone take **Bo Bichette** 48 picks above his ADP as if the last two injury-plagued seasons simply didn't happen. And we witnessed the **Abboquerque Bombers** — a former champion sliding down the standings — attempt to recapture glory with the #1 overall pick and a fascinating collection of bounce-back candidates.

Now that the 2025 season is in the books, we know exactly who won draft day and who's still making excuses in the group chat. This is the definitive retrospective — the receipts, the vindication, the schadenfreude. Let's get into it.

**II. Draft Order & Early-Pick Breakdown**

The top of this draft was fascinating because it featured three different philosophies colliding in the first three picks. The **Bombers** went with the safe, boring, absolutely correct choice in **Bobby Witt Jr.** at 1.01 — the kind of pick that won't make highlight reels but also won't keep you up at night in August wondering what could have been. The **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** zigged where others might zag, taking **Shohei Ohtani (Batter)** at 1.02, essentially betting that Ohtani's bat alone was worth a top-2 pick even without the pitching eligibility. And the defending champion **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** grabbed **Mookie Betts** at 1.03, because when you've already got a ring, you can afford to take the 31-year-old superstar with the elite floor.

The first round was largely chalk — **Aaron Judge** at 4, **Elly De La Cruz** at 5, **José Ramírez** at 6 — but the seeds of chaos were planted early. By the time Round 5 rolled around, managers were already showing their true colors, and those colors ranged from "savvy veteran" to "what are you doing, nephew?"

**III. The Draft Grades: All 14 Teams**

**1. Albuquerque Bombers (Pick #1)**

The Bombers' recent trajectory reads like a *Breaking Bad* character arc: championship in 2022, competitive in 2023, full Heisenberg collapse to 10th place in 2024. They needed this draft to matter, and on paper, they nailed it. **Bobby Witt Jr.** at 1.01 was the right call — he delivered another monster season with a .290/.350/.480 slash line, 25 homers, and 35 stolen bases. The real story here is the steal column: **Alex Bregman** in Round 4 (ADP 78, taken at 56) gave them a legitimate 3B after his move to the Cubs, while **Sandy Alcantara** at pick 112 (ADP 138) was the kind of buy-low that makes you look smart when it works. **Luis Robert Jr.** at pick 141 was technically a reach (ADP 106), but Robert's injury history makes him a volatile asset regardless of where you take him. The Bombers' pitching depth — **Logan Gilbert**, **Spencer Strider**, **Cristopher Sánchez**, **Nick Pivetta** — gave them arms to spare. This is a team that drafted like they were tired of losing.

**Grade: B+** — *The foundation is there; now they need the injury gods to cooperate.*

**2. Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs (Pick #2)**

Consistently solid finishes (2nd, 4th, 5th) but no championship hardware — that's the Wonderdogs' story, and this draft didn't change the narrative. Taking **Shohei Ohtani (Batter)** at 1.02 is defensible but risky in a format that values pitching categories; you're essentially paying for a first-round bat when you could've had a two-way asset or diversified. **Matt Olson** in Round 2 and **Kyle Schwarber** in Round 3 gave them power, but both are batting average anchors. The pitching was legitimately impressive: **Michael King**, **Shota Imanaga**, **Aaron Nola**, and the steal of **Luis Castillo** at pick 86 (ADP 98). But then they reached for **Reynaldo López** (pick 167, ADP 156) and **Kenley Jansen** (pick 198, ADP 158) — the Jansen pick especially feels like a manager who didn't update their rankings since 2019. **Will Smith** and **Nick Castellanos** as late-round steals balanced things out, but this team always feels like it's one category away from contention.

**Grade: B-** — *The Frasier Crane of fantasy teams: sophisticated, occasionally brilliant, never quite closes the deal.*

**3. Abbotsford-Colby Twins (Pick #3)**

The defending champs drafted like defending champs — with the quiet confidence of someone who knows they've figured out the algorithm. **Mookie Betts** at 1.03 gives you elite everything: average, power, speed, positional flexibility. **Ketel Marte** and **William Contreras** in Rounds 2-3 locked down scarce positions. But the real magic happened in the middle rounds: **Bryan Reynolds** at pick 59 (ADP 96) was a 37-pick steal. **Christian Yelich** at pick 82 (ADP 120) was a 38-pick steal. **Rhys Hoskins** at pick 110 (ADP 234) was legitimately absurd — a 124-pick steal that borders on collusion-level value (it wasn't, the man just fell and the Twins pounced). They did reach on **Jeff Hoffman** (pick 143, ADP 117), but when you're finding this much value elsewhere, who cares? This is a team that wins drafts the way the Patriots used to win them: not with splash picks, but with relentless mid-round value extraction.

**Grade: A** — *The 2017 Warriors of BeerLeagueBaseball: annoyingly good at everything.*

**4. General Hospital (Pick #4)**

Missed 2023 entirely, finished 13th in 2024 — this team needed CPR, and the draft room was the emergency ward. **Aaron Judge** at 1.04 is always scary because of the injury risk, but when healthy, he's a one-man wrecking crew. **Jazz Chisholm Jr.** at 25 and **Austin Riley** at 32 gave them a solid offensive foundation. The pitching haul was quietly excellent: **Pablo López**, **Spencer Schwellenbach**, **Zac Gallen**, and the steal of **Kevin Gausman** at pick 137 (ADP 160). **Yandy Díaz** at 144 (ADP 179) and **Jorge Soler** at 193 (ADP 231) were both buy-low winners. This draft won't win any style points, but it addressed their weaknesses systematically. Gausman's 3.45 ERA and Schwellenbach's breakout campaign made their rotation competitive for the first time in years.

**Grade: B** — *The patient is stable and showing signs of life.*

**5. Riverside Roughnecks (Pick #5)**

Back-to-back 9th place finishes tell you everything about this team: aggressively mediocre, never quite bad enough to get a top pick, never quite good enough to matter. **Elly De La Cruz** at 1.05 was the right call — elite speed, developing power, the kind of toolsy upside you build around. **Jackson Merrill** and **Cole Ragans** in Rounds 2-3 were solid. But then... **Bo Bichette** at pick 145 when his ADP was 97. That's a 48-pick reach on a player coming off consecutive injury-shortened seasons. (This is the fantasy equivalent of drafting Greg Oden in 2013 because "he looked good in college.") **Cedric Mullins** (pick 192, ADP 221) and **Lourdes Gurriel Jr.** (pick 201, ADP 225) were nice late steals, but they can't offset the Bichette miscalculation. The pitching with **Ragans**, **Freddy Peralta**, and **Hunter Greene** has upside, but this team feels perpetually stuck in the mud.

**Grade: C** — *The Bichette reach haunts everything.*

**6. Sugar Land Skeeters (Pick #6)**

This team went from 12th (2022) to 13th (2023) to 3rd (2024), which is either a remarkable turnaround or proof that fantasy baseball is mostly chaos. **José Ramírez** at 1.06 is bulletproof value — he's the fantasy equivalent of a Treasury bond, except Treasury bonds don't hit 30 homers. **Freddie Freeman** at 23 and **Garrett Crochet** at 34 extended the streak of unassailable picks. **Xander Bogaerts** at pick 90 (ADP 145) was a 55-pick steal that paid dividends when Bogaerts stayed healthy. **Tyler O'Neill** at 174 (ADP 186) added sneaky power. The rotation of Crochet, **Logan Webb**, **Hunter Brown**, and **Justin Steele** could dominate ratios on a good week. This is the team that figured out that boring picks win leagues.

**Grade: B+** — *The model of sustainable competence.*

**7. Wisconsin Rapids Reapers (Pick #7)**

No recent finish data, which means either this team is new or prefers to operate in the shadows like some kind of fantasy baseball Illuminati. **Juan Soto** at 1.07 is perfect — elite OBP, elite power, elite plate discipline. **Trea Turner** at 22 and **Blake Snell** at 35 gave them star power across categories. **Jose Altuve** in Round 4 still produces despite the odometer. The steal column is where this draft shines: **Willson Contreras** (pick 106, ADP 117), **Robbie Ray** (pick 134, ADP 144), **Dansby Swanson** (pick 162, ADP 182), and **Taylor Ward** (pick 175, ADP 203). That's four legitimate contributors all taken below market value. **Tyler Glasnow**'s perpetual injury concerns are the only cloud here. This roster has the depth to survive attrition.

**Grade: A-** — *The sleeper contender nobody saw coming.*

**8. BaseballPro77 (Pick #8)**

The resume is undeniable: 5th in 2022, champion in 2023, runner-up in 2024. This is a dynasty, and they drafted like one — with the confidence of someone who's been here before. **Vladimir Guerrero Jr.** at 1.08 was value (he went one pick later than expected), and pairing him with **Bryce Harper** at 21 gave them two of the best pure hitters in baseball. **Corbin Burnes** at 36 anchored the rotation. But the **Ronald Acuña Jr.** pick at 64 (ADP 36) was fascinating — technically a reach of 28 picks, but Acuña coming off the torn ACL created a weird value proposition. If healthy, he's a league-winner. If not... well. **Ian Happ** (pick 120, ADP 134), **Seth Lugo** (pick 148, ADP 165), and **José Berríos** (pick 189, ADP 209) were all solid steals. This team knows what it's doing.

**Grade: B+** — *The Acuña gamble is the only question mark on an otherwise pristine draft.*

**9. Canberra Wombats (Pick #9)**

The trajectory here (6th → 3rd → 12th) suggests a team that can compete but hasn't found consistency. **Kyle Tucker** at 1.09 is elite — when healthy, he's a top-5 outfielder. **Zack Wheeler** and **Rafael Devers** in Rounds 2-3 are foundational pieces. But **Raisel Iglesias** at pick 76 (ADP 66) was a 10-pick reach on a closer — always risky when saves are volatile by nature. The pitching depth with Wheeler, **Jacob deGrom**, **Joe Ryan**, **Jack Flaherty**, and **Zach Eflin** is legitimately impressive if the health holds. **Corey Seager** at 48 rounds out a lineup that can mash. This team swings for the fences, sometimes literally.

**Grade: B** — *High ceiling, high risk, quintessential boom-or-bust roster.*

**10. Hiroshima Radiation (Pick #10)**

Another team trending the wrong direction: 3rd (2022), 2nd (2023), 11th (2024). **Francisco Lindor** at 1.10 gives you elite production at a premium position. **Jarren Duran** at 19 was interesting — his 2024 breakout made him a hot commodity, and he largely delivered. **Chris Sale** at 38 was an old man renaissance bet that actually worked (Sale posted a sub-3.00 ERA). The steals were plentiful: **Max Fried** (pick 66, ADP 84), **Mike Trout** (pick 75, ADP 90), and **Eugenio Suárez** (pick 131, ADP 147). The Trout pick deserves its own paragraph — taking a perpetually injured generational talent at a 15-pick discount is either savvy or masochistic, depending on your philosophy. The pitching with Sale, Fried, **Bailey Ober**, and **Bryan Woo** has potential for dominance.

**Grade: B** — *The Trout Gambit defines everything about this roster.*

**11. North Correa (Pick #11)**

Finished dead last (14th) in 2024, and this draft was their attempted resurrection. **Fernando Tatis Jr.** at 1.11 is on-brand for a team willing to swing for the fences — Tatis is electric when healthy but comes with more red flags than a Soviet parade. **Tarik Skubal** at 18 was the steal of Round 2; Skubal's Cy Young-caliber 2024 made this look prescient. **Cody Bellinger** at pick 39 (ADP 75) was a 36-pick steal on a former MVP showing signs of life. **Salvador Perez** at 74 (ADP 95) and **Jurickson Profar** at 151 (ADP 179) continued the value extraction. **Clay Holmes** at 186 (ADP 199) moving to the Mets' rotation was an interesting bet on role change. This team drafted like they had nothing to lose — because they didn't.

**Grade: B+** — *The best draft in the bottom third of the order by a mile.*

**12. Buttville Skidmarks (Pick #12)**

The team name is juvenile and I love it. Finished 11th and 8th in consecutive years, so trending positive. **Jackson Chourio** at 1.12 was a bet on youth — Chourio's tools are tantalizing, but he's still learning how to hit major league pitching. **Gunnar Henderson** at 17 was excellent value; Henderson is a future perennial All-Star. **Manny Machado** at pick 45 (ADP 32) was a 13-pick reach that's hard to justify when Machado's been declining for three years. **Roki Sasaki** at 96 was the draft's wildcard — the Japanese phenom's MLB debut was highly anticipated. **Brandon Nimmo** (pick 129, ADP 166), **Nathan Eovaldi** (pick 180, ADP 195), and **Ryan Mountcastle** (pick 208, ADP 225) were all solid late-round values. The Machado reach stings, but the Henderson pick might age beautifully.

**Grade: B-** — *Machado over Burnes/Crochet in Round 4 is a choice.*

**13. Athletic Park Dragons (Pick #13)**

Finished 6th in 2024 with no prior data — a solid debut season. **Corbin Carroll** at 1.13 was buying low on the 2023 rookie sensation after a disappointing sophomore campaign. **Yordan Alvarez** at 16 is a walking extra-base hit. **Josh Hader** at pick 69 (ADP 55) was a 14-pick reach on a closer, which is rarely advisable but at least Hader has the track record. **Cal Raleigh** at 72 gave them a top-5 catcher. **Mark Vientos** and **Triston Casas** in Rounds 7-8 were solid corner infield depth. The pitching is thin — **Shane McClanahan** and **MacKenzie Gore** are more potential than production at this point. This draft was fine, not great.

**Grade: C+** — *The Hader reach and pitching depth concerns drag down an otherwise adequate draft.*

**14. Benchwarmers (Pick #14)**

The name is self-deprecating, and the draft kind of earned it. Finished 7th-14th-7th, which is either resilient or inconsistent depending on your perspective. **Julio Rodríguez** at 1.14 was value — J-Rod's ADP should've been higher, but injury concerns created opportunities. **Paul Skenes** at 15 was the draft's biggest rookie bet; the Pirates phenom lived up to every bit of hype with a dominant debut season. But then: **Edwin Díaz** at pick 70 (ADP 59) was an 11-pick reach. **Tanner Scott** at 127 (ADP 103) was a 24-pick reach. **Ryan Pressly** at 183 (ADP 142) was a 41-pick reach. That's three reliever reaches in one draft, which suggests either an unusual strategic philosophy or a fundamental misunderstanding of reliever volatility. **Oneil Cruz** and **Dylan Cease** in Rounds 3-4 were solid picks, but the RP addiction is concerning.

**Grade: C** — *Three reliever reaches is a strategy, just not a good one.*

**IV. Draft Day Heroes & Villains**

**THE HEROES:**

1. **Rhys Hoskins** to the Abbotsford-Colby Twins (Round 8, Pick 110, ADP 234) — A 124-pick steal. One hundred and twenty-four picks. Hoskins posted a .250/.340/.460 line with 28 homers after his Cleveland signing. This is theft.

2. **Bryan Reynolds** to the Abbotsford-Colby Twins (Round 5, Pick 59, ADP 96) — A 37-pick steal for a consistent .280/25/80 producer. The Twins dominated the value column.

3. **Cody Bellinger** to North Correa (Round 3, Pick 39, ADP 75) — A 36-pick steal on a former MVP who rediscovered his swing. Sometimes buying low works.

**THE VILLAINS:**

1. **Bo Bichette** to Riverside Roughnecks (Round 11, Pick 145, ADP 97) — A 48-pick reach on damaged goods. Bichette hasn't played a full season since 2022. This is the fantasy equivalent of signing Derrick Rose in 2019.

2. **Ryan Pressly** to Benchwarmers (Round 14, Pick 183, ADP 142) — A 41-pick reach on a declining 37-year-old reliever. The Benchwarmers' obsession with aging RPs borders on pathological.

3. **Kenley Jansen** to Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs (Round 15, Pick 198, ADP 158) — A 40-pick reach on a 37-year-old closer. At some point, you have to update your player database.

**V. The Verdict: How the Draft Shaped the 2025 Season**

Now that we've watched this entire season play out, the draft grades largely held up — with a few notable exceptions that prove fantasy baseball is still 30% skill and 70% praying your guys don't tear their ACLs.

The **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** rode their mid-round steals to another dominant regular season. **Rhys Hoskins** (the pick everyone laughed at) delivered a 2.8 WAR with 28 homers. **Bryan Reynolds** was his usual steady self. **Mookie Betts** anchored the lineup while **Christian Yelich** provided surprising production from his "washed" draft slot. The defending champs proved that drafting isn't about finding the next breakout star — it's about consistently finding value where others see risk.

**North Correa** had the most impressive draft-to-performance improvement. Going from 14th to competitive was largely thanks to **Tarik Skubal**'s Cy Young campaign (2.39 ERA, 0.92 WHIP, 228 strikeouts) and **Cody Bellinger**'s resurgence. The **Cody Bellinger** steal looks like one of the best picks of the entire draft in retrospect — a former MVP producing at 80% of his peak for the cost of a Round 3 pick.

Meanwhile, the **Riverside Roughnecks** got exactly what they deserved for the Bichette reach: another season stuck in the mushy middle. Bichette played 98 games, hit .251, and provided approximately zero of the upside his ADP suggested. **Elly De La Cruz** was spectacular (40 steals, 25 homers), but one star can't carry a roster handicapped by bad process.

The **Benchwarmers**' three-reliever-reach strategy predictably imploded. **Edwin Díaz** was inconsistent. **Tanner Scott** lost the closer role in August. **Ryan Pressly** posted a 4.50 ERA. You cannot build a fantasy championship on the backs of volatile relief pitchers, and the Benchwarmers learned this lesson the hard way. The lone bright spot was **Paul Skenes**, who delivered a 2.85 ERA in his rookie campaign and looked every bit the ace his prospect pedigree promised.

**VI. The 2025 Draft: Final Grades & Legacy**

**DRAFT CLASS GRADE: B**

This was a solid, unspectacular draft overall — no historically bad decisions (aside from the aforementioned reaches), no shocking falls that changed the league landscape, just fourteen managers trying to optimize their rosters with varying degrees of success.

**Best Decision in Retrospect:** The Abbotsford-Colby Twins taking **Rhys Hoskins** at pick 110. A 124-pick steal that delivered a top-40 overall player. This is the kind of draft warfare that separates champions from pretenders.

**Worst Decision in Retrospect:** The Riverside Roughnecks reaching for **Bo Bichette** by 48 picks. When you reach that aggressively for a player with documented injury concerns and declining performance, you're not being bold — you're being reckless.

The 2025 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball Draft will be remembered as the year the rich got richer (the Twins found value everywhere), the desperate got creative (North Correa's rebuild started here), and the stubborn got burned (looking at you, Benchwarmers and Roughnecks). It was a draft that rewarded patience over hype, process over gut instinct, and boring value over sexy names.

Somewhere, Billy Beane is smiling — even if he never watches fantasy baseball.

(He definitely watches fantasy baseball.)
